settlements and rights our people have fought decades to
secure. The Crown is
attempting to legislate its way out of solemn promises made to our people,
and we won't stand idly by
while the settlement we ne-negotiated in good faith
is treated as a secondary consideration to a new
bureaucracy.
Now, the next point I'd like to talk to is what I term the partnership
gap.
Within our written submission, our first overarching concern is the language of the
bills.
Contrary to popular belief, Māori are partners of the
Crown, yet these bills relegate us to mere participants
in decision-making.
Mm.
This is a fundamental downgrade, and furthermore, there's no overarching
commitment to Te Tiriti. Unlike the Resource
Management Act, which at least required the Crown to take into account treaty
principles, these new bills offer no such
directive. This lack of a treaty
clause
creates a massive legal vacuum. Without
a clear requirement to give effect to Te Tiriti, the Crown risks
continuing a cycle of breaches
that undermine Māori tino rangatiratanga
and our role as tangata whenua with our res- within our respective
rohe.
Point number three I'd like to touch on
is what we see as a, a threat to our
settlements. The Resource Management Act
is not an isolated piece of law.
It's deeply integrated into our settlement
redress. Any change to the
RMA has an enduring impact on the guaranteed rights
of Te Pumautanga o Te Arawa.
Specifically, we're concerned with Section 9.

includes the phrase "to the greatest extent
possible under these bills". This, we
believe, is a, is an escape hatch, a get out of
jail free card for the Crown. It suggests that the
provisions of these new bills
can override existing settlement acts.
The Crown cannot be allowed to use new legislation
to excuse itself from the promises it made in good faith
during settlement negotiations.
We refer you specifically to Part
4 of the Affiliate Te Arawa Iwi Hapū Deed of
Settlement. In that deed, the
Crown explicitly agreed to give effect to the settlement through
legislation that is s-satisfactory to both the
Crown and
Te Pumautanga o Te Arawa. By forcing
these bills
through without our consent, and by allowing these
bills to override settlement obligations,
we believe the Crown is in direct breach of its contract with the Te Arawa
affiliates
that negotiated our existing deed of settlement.
You, you can't use new law to denigrate old
promises.And the
final point, point I'd like to touch on relates to shifting
the burden of transition
and the two-year,
trap.
You know, le-let's talk about the cost of this transition.
The Crown
is asking us to do its work for
it. The Crown is asking us to spend our
own time and money on legal experts and technical advice
just to preserve rights we already
own. Under Section 9, we're
expected to review, negotiate, and redraft agreements
to ensure our rights aren't
lost. But where's the
resourcing?
The Crown has provided limited funding
that doesn't even begin to cover the specialist legal and technical advice
required to navigate this mess.
Furthermore, the two-year cap on, negotiations
is an insult. Time is our enemy
here, and time is,
is, is an asset that we've
already dealt with
to achieve this, this settlement.
So the Crown expects to renegotiate every settlement a-arrangement in the country
in 24 months. This is a setup for
failure. It creates a ticking clock where
if an agreement isn't reached, the Crown can simply shrug
its shoulders and move on,
leaving our rights in the dust. This isn't
engagement, it's a forced march.
In addition, Section 10 offers no clarity on
how equivalent effect will be maintained during this
period. We need clear
procedural mechanisms developed in partnership
to ensure we aren't left in a legal limbo.
Now, I've only really touched on some of the points from our written submission
that we believe need to be considered
while we have your ear.
So with that, we have some proposed amendments to remedy the
failings that I've alluded to in my oral
submission. Te Pumautanga o Te
Arawa submits the following essential
amendments. Number one,
removing the limiting language. Strike out to the
greatest extent possible from Sections 9 and
10. The law must state that settlements will be
upheld, irā kati. Full stop.
Number two,
ensure no diminishment.
Include a positive obligation on the Crown
to ensure that the effect of any settlement is not diminished by
this transition.
Number three, provide resourcing.
Mandate funding for PSGs to participate in these complex
negotiations. Number four,
fix the timeline.
Remove the arbitrary two-year cap.
Rights don't have an expiry date, and neither should the
Crown's obligation to protect them.
So fix the timeline to allow for meaningful
engagement.
So look, in closing, Te
Pumautanga o Te Arawa cannot support a regime that pri-prioritizes
administrative ease
over treaty justice.
The Crown is currently failing to u-uphold its
obligations. If you pass these bills in their current
form, you are not just, you are not just reforming the
RMA, you are committing a fresh breach of Te
Tiriti. The Te Arawa affiliates negotiated
in good faith.
We expect the Crown to do the same.
We do not expect a same or equivalent effect
that is determined solely by the Crown.
We ask this select committee
to uphold the integrity of our settlements and ensure that any
new, environment framework begins with a true
partnership, not a step backward.
We demand a partnership that respects the sanctity of our
settlement. Uh, ka tíra i konei, e te
tiamana, e te komiti whakahaere. Uh, nei rā
anō, te mihi atu ki a koutou. Uh, nō
reira, tēnā koutou, tēnā koutou, o tērā, tēnā
tātou. Thank you.
Thank you very much. I appreciate your, presentation.
Thank you very much, and Lan Pham has a question.
Tēnā koe, Matua Clark.
Kia ora.
Thank you so much for that very clear submission.
Um,
one thing that we've been trying to get a,
a, I guess, understanding of as a committee is
in the lead up to these bills
actually, you know, being notified and, and
being open for submissions, what was your
engagement, with officials prior
to that for Te Arawa Trust?
Um, so we were, engaged,
with, MFE. So, we, we did
speak, I guess our truth in terms
of,
what these bills mean to us and the potential impact.
So we have had discussions with, our, our office
has had discussions with, the Ministry for the Environment.
Um, so that has happened, which I do
appreciate.Um,
I, I, I think some of
our leanings, have kind of
not been included in, you know, in what we currently have,
in terms of the two bills that are,
are before us at the moment.
Mm. Thank you. Can I ask another question, Chair?
Yes, sure. Fire away, love.
Um, 'cause that is, I guess, the feedback that we've often
heard, particularly from, iwi, hapū
and settlement groups. Um, they've had some form of

What hope do you have for, for example, this
two-year timeline that the bill sets out to
kind of resolve, settlement
issues that, I guess, the, your voice will be
heard in that timeframe?
I, I guess the reality for us is
if,
if this two-year,
cap is binding, and it's not gonna move,
then
it kind of makes sense that, extra resourcing
is, is accorded us
so that we can meet that, that really tight, tight
timeframe.
Um, and to be honest, even if we are fully resourced, I
think it'll still be a struggle to, meet that
two-year, cap because we're
one of
however many other settlements are already in play.
So,
yeah, it, it, it is, it is a concern.
It's still a concern.
Ngā mihi.
Yep.
Well, look, thank you very much, Clark. That was great.
Um, really much appreciate your presentation.
Um, we've, come to the end of our, of your time, so thank you very
much once again. Appreciate it very much, and have a, have a great day.
Cheers. Thank you.
Kia ora.
Kia ora. And we come to our final,
our final submitter, Palmerston North City Council.
Uh, Chair-
I'm so pleased. Um, you don't know how pleased we all are to see this submission.
'Cause it is the last one of the day, so it's been- ...
oh, no, three days in a row, I might point out.
Um, so no, th- look, seriously, thank you very much.
Uh, really, really appreciate you, committing, coming in today to submit to

Thank you.
Uh, kia ora Chair, and thank you for hearing us, and, we'll, we'll try

introduce ourselves first. I'm Lorna Johnson.
I'm an elected member at Palmerston North City Council and an RMA

Uh, kia ora koutou. Uh, David Murphy, uh General Manager of Strategic

Good afternoon, everyone. Uh, Jono Ferguson-Pike, City Planning Manager at

shift planning away from local control to centralized

elected representatives and local communities in shaping plans.

there's been, particular environments, I'm particularly thinking of a recent

though it would technically be a greenfield, plan, there

So we're a bit concerned about centralized direction not being able

Um, and in particular I would say, as an elected member, I'm concerned that

Um, and we're also worried that if the minister has a large amount of

Over to you, David.
Thank you, Lorna. Yeah, just, just so I guess to build on those points that,

trigger, under this system, a nationwide
response to that national direction across all the regional spatial

again, we support standardization of a number of planning provisions
a-across the country. Uh, but I, I don't say this lightly, I think

users, the council, a-and, and, and, and being put in place.

Yeah, thanks, David. Um, hi, everyone.
Yeah, look, really what I wanna talk about is sort of

Um, no doubt you've, you've heard about a lot of those, those issues,

and obviously, that, that is something that we would

we have a lot of growth plan changes, probably of...

community. Um, and so I wanted to ask you

Yeah. Look, I mean, that's a r-a really good example, and SNAs have been

I guess what I would say is, has there's been, some,

of, pieces of the environment, you know, signi-
requires a huge amount of good quality information, and it

Yes

Absolutely. I think more time is required to, to get this

Yeah. I, I agree with that. I guess the, what I, what I would add is any new system

Uh, so a bit more time getting the legislation correct is one thing, but I

create standard rules for the whole country in the space of, I
think 12 to 15 months, when these plans have
taken six to seven, eight years of negotiation at a local level to play out
is just unrealistic.
Look, thank you. Look, hey, we've reached time. Thank you very much.
Really appreciate yours and your very fulsome submission.
Uh, thank you, and, that's great, and have a great day down there in Palmerston
North. Cheers.
Okay. See you.
Cheers. Thank you for your time
